goal: Quantify the impact of Laser Powder Bed Fusion (LPBF) processing parameters—specifically scan speed and laser power—on the porosity-induced fatigue life of Inconel 718. Compare these findings with Electron Beam Melting (EBM) benchmarks at elevated temperatures (650°C), citing primary data from technical manufacturing repositories.

Current findings provide a solid foundation for LPBF Inconel 718 fatigue mechanisms at 650°C, specifically regarding melt pool boundaries and in-situ re-scanning effects. However, the knowledge graph

Current data provides a solid foundation for LPBF Inconel 718 fatigue mechanisms and surface enhancement at 650°C, but EBM-specific benchmarks remain under-represented. While we have identified that s

Current findings establish that surface machining improves LPBF Inconel 718 fatigue strength by ~50% at 650°C and that EBM post-processing reduces property variance. However, specific numerical S-N da

Current findings establish that surface polishing enhances SLM Inconel 718 fatigue strength by ~50% at 650°C, but specific quantitative S-N data for EBM benchmarks remains sparse. While EBM mechanical

Current findings establish the general mechanisms of Shot Peening (SP) and Ultrasonic Nanocrystal Surface Modification (UNSM) in improving fatigue life through compressive residual stresses and grain

Research has confirmed that both Shot Peening and UNSM induce compressive residual stresses and work hardening in Inconel 718, effectively delaying crack initiation. However, specific quantitative ben

Current data identifies significant fatigue life improvements (up to 56-fold for UNSM) in AM Inconel 718, but lacks the specific 'Thermal Half-Life'—the time-dependent decay rate of these benefits at
Wrote section on: Investigate the specific parameters used

user_steers.steer: Deep-scrape technical repositories for EBM Inconel 718 fatigue benchmarks at 650°C.
Focus on gathering data related to EBM Inconel 718 fatigue performance at 650°C, specifically prioritizing data concerning surface characteristics and post-processing techniques.
Deep-scrape EBM-specific fatigue datasets at 650°C from technical repositories (TMS, NASA, ScienceDirect).
Investigate the impact of surface polishing and shot peening on the fatigue life of EBM Inconel 718 at 650°C, specifically focusing on how these techniques mitigate surface-initiated fatigue.

Investigate the application and effectiveness of Ultrasonic Nanocrystal Surface Modification (UNSM) on the fatigue life of EBM Inconel 718 at 650°C, including its impact on surface characteristics and mitigation of surface-initiated fatigue.
Gather comparative metrics for fatigue life improvement percentages when using UNSM versus traditional shot peening on EBM Inconel 718 at 650°C.
Determine the 'Thermal Half-Life' of fatigue life improvement in EBM Inconel 718 at 650°C, specifically focusing on the degradation of surface treatments like UNSM and shot peening over time.

Continue gathering data on comparative metrics for fatigue life improvement percentages when using UNSM versus traditional shot peening on EBM Inconel 718 at 650°C, the 'Thermal Half-Life' of fatigue life improvement in EBM Inconel 718 at 650°C, and quantifying Thermal Relaxation and Half-Life.
Investigate the specific parameters used in shot peening processes for EBM Inconel 718, particularly those affecting fatigue life at 650°C, including peening intensity, media type, and coverage.

options_presented.text: Extract kinetic relaxation models (e.g., Zener-Wertman) for residual stresses in Inconel 718 at 650°C to calculate 'Thermal Half-Life'.
Perform a targeted search for EBM-specific high-temperature (650°C) fatigue datasets comparing UNSM and Shot Peening.
Analyze the relationship between surface roughness (Ra) reduction by UNSM versus Shot Peening and its impact on crack initiation at 650°C.

outputs_written.topic: Investigate the specific parameters used in shot peening processes for EBM Inconel 718, particularly those affecting fatigue life at 650°C, including peening intensity, media type, and coverage.
outputs_written.text: Shot peening (SP) serves as a critical surface enhancement for Inconel 718 components, particularly those operating at the 650°C threshold where the alloy maintains excellent creep-rupture properties [1, 2]. By inducing residual compressive stresses, SP parameters such as intensity and coverage are designed to delay fatigue crack initiation and hinder small crack propagation [3]. However, the effectiveness of these parameters is inextricably linked to the material's initial microstructural state

gaps: Quantitative 'Thermal Half-Life' values (time to 50% loss of fatigue benefit) for UNSM vs. Shot Peening at 650°C.
Direct S-N curve comparisons for EBM Inconel 718 at 650°C specifically comparing UNSM and Shot Peening treatments.
Empirical correlation between post-treatment surface roughness (Ra) and the high-temperature fatigue limit for EBM-manufactured parts.
